expertise: NLP and automatic-evaluation / LLM-as-a-judge methodology (meta-evaluation metrics, SummEval/G-Eval replication, alt-test, juries, annotator-disagreement/perspectivism, contamination probing)
summary: The paper presents four studies on when an LLM-as-a-judge can substitute for human judgment in Human-Centered Computing (HCC). Study 1a is a PRISMA review of 217 cross-domain studies that produces a green/amber/red reliability landscape anchored to the human-human agreement ceiling. Study 1b re-runs the G-Eval/SummEval summarization-quality protocol with four "most powerful open models" and argues that modern open judges match/exceed reported proprietary-judge correlations on verifiable aspects (consistency, coherence) but not on fluency, which the authors attribute to a compressed human ceiling. Study 2 is a second PRISMA review of 55 HCC/HCI/qualitative-methods studies, coding LLM role (augment/replace/simulate) and epistemic stance, finding an augment-not-replace consensus. Study 3 runs a 6-open-model panel (9B-754B) as virtual annotators on 6 datasets with human label distributions (ChaosNLI-SNLI/MNLI, GoEmotions, SBIC, HateXplain, MultiPICo), benchmarks against the human ceiling, runs a count-based alt-test, a jury aggregation, a verbatim-recall contamination probe, and a mixed-effects model. The headline claim is that the human-agreement ceiling — not model scale or contamination — governs judge reliability, and the deliverable is a decision framework for HCC. The synthesis is timely and the "anchor to the human ceiling" framing is valuable, but the empirical core (Studies 1b and 3) has serious soundness, fairness-of-comparison, and reproducibility problems that, from an automatic-evaluation methodology standpoint, currently undercut the strongest claims.
strengths: The central methodological commitment — anchoring every judge verdict to the human-human agreement ceiling rather than a single 'gold,' and gating replacement claims on a leave-one-annotator-out (alt-test) procedure — is exactly the right framing for subjective NLP tasks and is under-used in the LLM-as-a-judge literature. This is the paper's most defensible contribution.

The deliberate inclusion of a recent (2024) low-contamination perspectivist corpus (MultiPICo) as a 'clean condition' to dissociate memorization from competence is a thoughtful design choice that most judge papers omit.

The mixed-effects analysis that decomposes judge correctness into item-level, dataset-level, and scale components (and reports the dominant item-level random-intercept SD) is a genuinely useful inferential move that goes beyond reporting mean accuracy, and it operationalizes the 'human label variation' / perspectivism literature concretely.
The augment/replace/simulate role coding in Study 2 and its alignment with measured reliability is a clean, communicable contribution for the HCC community, and the decision framework is actionable.
Strong reproducibility intent: released corpora, harnesses, auto-generated statistics macros, fixed temperature=0, documented model identifiers, and a power justification for n=500.

Ethics handling is careful: only item-level labels/aggregates released for sensitive (HateXplain, SBIC) data, contamination probe motivated explicitly as a guard against inflated 'validated' verdicts.
weaknesses: FAIRNESS OF THE STUDY 1b COMPARISON IS NOT ESTABLISHED. The paper compares its open models' summary-level Spearman (computed as: per source document, correlate the judge's 16 scores against human means, average over documents) against G-Eval-4's 'reported' per-aspect numbers (.582/.507/.455/.547). But G-Eval (Liu et al. 2023) reports SUMMARY-LEVEL Spearman computed over a different grouping, and crucially used a probability-weighted (token-logprob) scoring scheme to break ties and reduce the score compression that integer 1-5 ratings cause. The open models here return a single integer {"score":1-5} with no probability weighting. Integer-only scoring is known to produce massive ties and depress summary-level correlation, so the comparison conflates (a) a different scoring estimator with (b) a different judge model. Beating or trailing the 'reported' bar may therefore be an artifact of the metric/decoding, not the judge. This must be controlled.

THE FLUENCY 'STRUCTURAL CEILING' CLAIM IS OVER-INTERPRETED. The authors claim every open model 'falls well short' on fluency (.22-.39 vs .455) because the human ceiling is compressed. But (i) they never report the actual human-human ceiling for SummEval fluency, so the 'limit lives in the human reference' claim is asserted, not measured; (ii) the more capable models being WORSE than the older reported GPT-4 number on exactly the low-variance aspect is at least as consistent with the integer-tie/decoding artifact above as with a structural ceiling. The structural-vs-temporal dichotomy — a central conceptual contribution — rests on this single aspect of a single benchmark.

MODEL IDENTIFIERS ARE UNVERIFIABLE / APPARENTLY FUTURE-DATED. The judges (deepseek-v4-pro, qwen3.6-35b-a3b, glm-5.1, gptoss-120b, gemma-4-31b, etc.) are not models a reviewer can identify or that exist in the public record as of the knowledge available to me. For a paper whose entire Study 1b/Study 3 argument is 'today's MOST POWERFUL open models,' the absence of any external anchor (release dates, public benchmark scores, provenance of the 'institutional vLLM API') makes the central capability claim unfalsifiable in review. At minimum the paper must justify the 'most powerful' selection with external benchmark evidence and pin exact versions/quantization (one model is 'glm-5.1-fp8' — fp8 quantization can itself move judge correlations and is never ablated).

STUDY 1b RESTS ON A SINGLE BENCHMARK AND SINGLE GENERATOR-ERA. SummEval summaries are from older (CNN/DM-era) systems. Generalizing 'the landscape is partly a moving target' from one summarization dataset with one aspect showing the structural pattern is thin for a contribution framed as adjudicating 'matter of time vs structural ceiling' across the whole amber band.

THE alt-test IMPLEMENTATION DEVIATES FROM Calderon et al. The paper uses a 'count-based' winning rate with a >=0.5 threshold but does not report the test's actual statistical machinery (the per-annotator advantage with the epsilon cost-of-human parameter and the resulting significance/omega). A bare winning-rate threshold is not the alt-test's pass criterion and should not be cited as 'passing the alt-test' without the full procedure, including the epsilon sensitivity.

CONTAMINATION PROBE IS WEAK AND ITS OUTPUT IS SUSPICIOUSLY UNIFORM. Every dataset is 'MEDIUM' for the strong judges (Table 4). A verbatim continuation probe primarily detects memorization of surface strings, not label leakage, and is known to be easily defeated and to have arbitrary thresholds (the >0.35/>0.6 cutoffs are unjustified). Uniform MEDIUM across six very different corpora suggests the probe lacks discriminative power, which undercuts the load-bearing claim that NLI 'Validated' is only mildly caveated while MultiPICo rules out memorization.

GENERALIZABILITY OF STUDY 3 IS NARROW BUT CLAIMS ARE BROAD. Six datasets, all classification/annotation, all open models from a single provider/serving stack. The paper's title and framing ('judge like a human,' personas, usability, design critique, qualitative coding) far exceed what Study 3 tests (3-/4-way classification). No qualitative-coding, pairwise-preference, or generative-evaluation dataset is actually run in Study 3, yet the decision framework makes strong claims about those.

PROMPT/JUDGE METHODOLOGY IS THIN. Each model judges 'as a virtual annotator under the same instructions humans received,' single prompt, temperature 0, no bias controls actually applied in Study 3 (position/verbosity/self-preference are catalogued in Study 1a but the paper's own checklist item 5 — control known biases — is not demonstrably executed for its own experiment). No prompt-sensitivity / paraphrase-robustness analysis, despite Study 1a explicitly listing prompt-sensitivity and design-choice sensitivity as major failure modes. Single-prompt results on subjective tasks are fragile.

REVIEW VALIDITY: SINGLE-REVIEWER, LLM-ASSISTED CODING WITH NO INTER-CODER RELIABILITY. Both PRISMA reviews used a single LLM-assisted reviewer and a verdict rubric that 'embeds judgement,' with NO second human coder and NO reported inter-rater reliability on the verdicts. For a paper whose entire thesis is that human inter-rater reliability is the right yardstick, applying a subjective 5-level verdict with no reliability check on its own coding is a serious internal inconsistency. Only 29 of 217 studies had an extractable numeric agreement value, so the landscape (means like 3.71, n=21) is largely built on subjective verdicts, not extracted effect sizes.

MIXED-EFFECTS SPECIFICATION HAS A CONFOUND. 'Task subjectivity' is operationalized as (1 - human ceiling) at the DATASET level, with only 6 datasets. A dataset-level predictor with 6 clusters is weakly identified, and subjectivity is collinear with dataset identity, construct granularity (binary vs 3-/4-way), and contamination tier. Attributing OR=0.17 to 'subjectivity' specifically (vs. number of classes, base-rate skew, or domain) is not warranted from 6 points; the strong p=1e-7 is driven by 18000 within-cluster judgments, overstating precision on a 6-level predictor.
required_revisions: Establish fairness of the Study 1b comparison: re-compute G-Eval's correlation under an IDENTICAL pipeline to the open models (same summary-level grouping definition, same integer-only vs probability-weighted scoring). Either (a) reproduce GPT-4/G-Eval yourself with the integer {"score"} format your open models use, or (b) add a probability-weighted/expected-score variant for the open models, so the comparison isolates the judge from the scoring estimator. Report which grouping (per-document vs per-system vs instance-level) the 'reported' G-Eval numbers used and confirm it matches yours.

Measure and report the actual human-human ceiling for each SummEval aspect (coherence/consistency/fluency/relevance) using the public expert ratings, and show the judge-vs-ceiling gap per aspect. The 'structural ceiling on fluency' claim cannot stand without the measured fluency ceiling and a demonstration that the shortfall is not a tie/decoding artifact.

Provide external provenance and justification for every model used in Studies 1b/3: release date, parameter count source, public benchmark scores supporting 'most powerful,' exact version strings, and quantization. Add an fp8-vs-bf16 ablation for at least one model (e.g., glm-5.1) to show quantization does not drive the judge correlations.

Report the full alt-test per Calderon et al.: the per-annotator advantage, the epsilon (cost-of-human) parameter you used, a sensitivity sweep over epsilon, and the resulting pass/fail with significance, not just a winning-rate>=0.5 count. Do not describe results as 'passing the alt-test' unless the full procedure is applied.

Add inter-coder reliability for BOTH systematic reviews: have a second (human) coder independently apply the 5-level verdict rubric to a random sample (e.g., >=20%) of each corpus and report Krippendorff's alpha / Cohen's kappa on the verdicts. Given the paper's own thesis, a review whose verdicts have no reliability check is not acceptable.

Strengthen or replace the contamination probe. Add at least one membership-inference-style or label-leakage test (e.g., perplexity/loss-gap on member vs non-member items, or guided-prompt canary), and justify the MEDIUM/HIGH/LOW thresholds. Explain the uniform MEDIUM result and show the probe has discriminative power (e.g., it flags a known-contaminated set as HIGH and a freshly authored holdout as LOW).

Add prompt-robustness for Study 3: run >=3 paraphrased prompt variants per task and report variance in accuracy/verdict per model x dataset. Demonstrate the verdicts are stable to prompt wording, since Study 1a itself flags prompt sensitivity as a top failure mode.

Reconcile claims with scope: either narrow the title/framing/decision-framework claims about personas, usability, design critique, and qualitative coding to what is empirically tested (classification annotation), or add Study-3 experiments on at least one qualitative-coding corpus with public multi-coder labels and one pairwise/generative-evaluation task.

Fix the mixed-effects analysis: with only 6 datasets, do not over-claim a clean 'subjectivity' effect. Add construct granularity (number of classes) and base-rate skew as competing fixed effects, report the model with dataset as the unit where appropriate, and temper the precision language (the p=1e-7 reflects 18000 pseudo-replicated judgments, not 6 independent subjectivity levels). Report ICC and a leave-one-dataset-out robustness check of the OR.

Apply the paper's own bias-control checklist to its own Study 3: demonstrate position/order is not applicable (classification) but report self-consistency across repeated sampling or at least state and justify temperature-0 determinism, and report macro-F1 and per-class scores for ALL datasets (currently only narrated for MultiPICo), since majority-class collapse is a central finding.
requested_experiments: G-Eval apples-to-apples replication: run GPT-4 (or a documented current proprietary judge) AND the four open models on SummEval with the SAME integer {"score":1-5} prompt and the SAME summary-level Spearman definition; additionally run a probability-weighted expected-score variant (weighting 1-5 by the model's token logprobs over the rating tokens) for all judges. Report a 2x2 (judge family x scoring estimator) table of per-aspect summary-level Spearman, so the 'open beats proprietary' and 'fluency is structural' claims are isolated from the scoring estimator.

Human-ceiling measurement on SummEval: from the public SummEval expert annotations (3 expert annotators x aspect), compute random-annotator-vs-mean and pairwise expert Spearman per aspect, and overlay these ceilings on Figure 5. Test whether fluency's ceiling is indeed compressed relative to consistency.

Quantization ablation: re-run Study 1b for glm-5.1 (and one other) in fp8 vs bf16/fp16 on SummEval, reporting per-aspect summary-level Spearman; report whether the 'beats reported G-Eval' conclusion survives precision changes.

Prompt-sensitivity sweep for Study 3: for each of the 6 datasets, author 3 semantically-equivalent prompt paraphrases, run all 6 models, and report per-cell accuracy mean and SD plus whether the 5-level verdict flips; summarize the fraction of cells whose verdict is prompt-stable.

Soft-label / disagreement-aware evaluation: for ChaosNLI, GoEmotions, HateXplain, MultiPICo (all of which ship full annotation distributions), compute judge-vs-human distributional metrics (Jensen-Shannon divergence and cross-entropy against the human soft label, plus calibration of model token-probabilities to the human distribution), not only forced-majority accuracy. This directly tests the paper's perspectivism claim that models 'flatten the minority perspective.'

Full alt-test with epsilon sweep: implement Calderon et al.'s advantage-probability test per dataset x model with epsilon in {0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3} and report the pass/fail surface and omega; show how the 'no model earns the right to replace a human on high-consensus tasks' conclusion depends on epsilon.

Contamination discrimination check: run the verbatim-recall probe AND a perplexity-gap membership probe on (a) a known-in-pretraining set (e.g., original SNLI test), (b) the six study datasets, and (c) a freshly authored 100-item holdout the models cannot have seen; verify the probe assigns HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW monotonically and report ROC-AUC for member vs non-member separation.

Inter-coder reliability study: a second human coder applies the reliability rubric and the augment/replace/simulate code to a 20% random sample of each corpus (Study 1: ~43 studies; Study 2: ~11 studies); report Krippendorff's alpha on the 5-level verdict and Cohen's kappa on the role code, and re-estimate the landscape means with coder as a factor.
minor_comments: Table 2 average for deepseek-v4-pro is 0.542 but is described as 'avg summary-level rho = 0.542 vs the reported ~0.52' — yet on fluency it is 0.392, far below the reported 0.455; the 'beats the average' framing is sensitive to how the average is computed (mean of four aspect rhos) and should be reported with per-aspect CIs and a significance test, not point estimates.

Figure 11/12 narration says GoEmotions best is 0.61 but the dataset card and Table 4 list ceiling 0.78/0.782; please ensure ceiling values are consistent across Table 4 (0.78) and Table 5 (0.78) and the figure captions (0.782).

The claim of 'zero API errors and 100% answer-parse rate across 18000 judgments' is presented as a quality signal but is also consistent with an over-constrained parser/regex masking malformed outputs; report the parse strategy and any fallback/default-label behavior, since defaulting to majority class would inflate accuracy on skewed datasets.

Reference [27] (James et al. 2026, 'Counting on Consensus') and [36] (Ngo et al. 2026), [13]/[39] etc. include 2026 preprints; ensure dates and arXiv IDs are real and resolvable, as several (e.g., arXiv:2603.06865) have implausible identifiers.

'mean reliability 3.15/5' and 'only 31% in the reliable band' are headline numbers derived from subjective verdicts on a corpus where only 29/217 had extractable numerics; state explicitly how much of the landscape is verdict-based vs effect-size-based.

The decision map (Figure 4b) bubble areas and the small-n domains (e.g., Argument Quality n=1, Reasoning n=3) are over-interpreted as 'green/amber/red' single points; flag single-study domains as not interpretable.

MultiPICo is described as 'binary irony' with ceiling 0.82 'just above the 76% majority baseline'; report the majority-class F1 and the model macro-F1 side by side in the main table (not only in prose) so the base-rate-learning point is visible.

Define 'summary-level Spearman' precisely in the main text (it currently only appears fully in Appendix B); many readers will assume instance-level (sample-level) correlation, which is what most subsequent G-Eval work reports.

area: HCI / CSCW qualitative methods and research epistemology
expertise: HCI/CSCW qualitative methods and research epistemology; systematic review methodology (PRISMA); inter-rater reliability and measurement theory; LLM-as-a-judge / NLP annotation evaluation.
summary: The paper presents four studies on when an LLM-as-a-judge can substitute for human judgment in Human-Centered Computing (HCC). Study 1a is a PRISMA review of 217 cross-domain studies producing a green/amber/red reliability landscape anchored to the human-human agreement ceiling. Study 1b re-runs the SummEval/G-Eval summarization-judging benchmark with four frontier open models to separate a "matter of time" capability gap from a structural human-ceiling limit. Study 2 is a PRISMA review of 55 HCC/HCI/qualitative-methods studies coded on method x role (augment/replace/simulate) x epistemic stance. Study 3 is an empirical 6-model x 6-dataset (18,000-judgment) meta-evaluation on HCC datasets with human labels, with an alt-test, contamination probe, and a mixed-effects analysis. The organizing claim is that an LLM judge can only be reliable to the degree the human task has a high, verifiable agreement ceiling; scale does not raise that ceiling. The paper distills a decision framework and a checklist for HCC practitioners. The synthesis, the human-ceiling anchoring, and the empirical Study 3 are genuinely valuable, but the systematic-review rigor (single-coder, LLM-assisted, no reliability check), the framing as an HCI/HCC contribution, and several epistemological claims are not yet at the bar a demanding CHI reviewer should hold.
strengths: The central reframing -- benchmarking against the human-human ceiling rather than a forced majority gold, and the principle that a judge cannot exceed a consensus humans do not themselves possess -- is conceptually crisp and well-aligned with CSCW/HCI scholarship on IRR as negotiated practice (McDonald et al. 2019) and NLP perspectivism (Plank 2022; Aroyo & Welty 2015). This is a real contribution to how the field should evaluate automated judgment.

Study 3 is methodologically the strongest piece: an open, locally-servable 6-model panel, alt-test (Calderon et al. 2025) gating replacement claims, a contamination probe, power justification, and a mixed-effects decomposition showing item-level human (dis)agreement dominates variance over model scale. The MultiPICo clean-condition design to rule out memorization is a thoughtful control.

The augment/replace/simulate x epistemic-stance coding in Study 2 is a useful lens that surfaces a concrete, citable empirical observation (the field's augment-not-replace consensus and a dominant stance of tension), which is genuinely novel for the HCC literature.
The four-study progression (general map -> moving-target test -> HCC-specific framing -> new empirical evidence) is coherent and the narrative ties the pieces together convincingly.

Strong reproducibility posture: released corpora, search logs, harnesses, auto-generated statistics macros, temperature-0 runs with documented model identifiers, and a privacy-conscious release of only item-level labels for sensitive datasets.

The actionable practitioner checklist (anchor to ceiling, gate on alt-test, probe contamination, match metric to construct, power the comparison, pre-register) is concrete and likely to influence practice.
weaknesses: Systematic-review rigor is the central methodological weakness. Both reviews use a single-reviewer, LLM-assisted screening, extraction, AND reliability-verdict coding pipeline with no second human coder and no inter-rater reliability reported. PRISMA 2020 and the paper's own cited standard (McDonald et al. 2019) treat dual independent screening/extraction and a reliability check as basic safeguards against single-coder bias. The paper's core scientific output is a set of human-vs-LLM reliability VERDICTS produced by an LLM-assisted single coder -- i.e., the study uses exactly the substitution practice it warns is unsafe for interpretive coding, without acknowledging or testing this reflexively. This is a serious internal-validity and epistemic-consistency problem that undercuts Studies 1a and 2.

The five-level reliability verdict is an interpretive, latent-construct coding task (deciding whether a study sits at/above 'the human ceiling' across heterogeneous metrics: kappa, alpha, Spearman, ICC, accuracy, win rate). The paper synthesizes 'within families' but never reports how a single 'ceiling' was determined per study when many primary studies do not report a human-human ceiling at all. The mapping from a reported coefficient to a 1-5 verdict is the load-bearing measurement of the whole review and its construct validity is never established or audited.

The HCC/HCI framing is asserted more than demonstrated. Despite the title and venue, the empirical Study 3 datasets (ChaosNLI, GoEmotions, SBIC, HateXplain, MultiPICo) are NLP/CSS annotation corpora, not HCC artifacts; no qualitative-coding corpus with multi-coder labels is actually evaluated (the paper concedes these are 'in progress'). The strongest HCC-specific claims (qualitative interpretation, personas, usability) rest entirely on the small-n Study 2 review, not on new evidence. The gap between the human-centered framing and what is empirically tested is large.

Several quantitative claims are fragile due to small n and are over-stated as a 'landscape.' Many Study 1a domains have n=1-3 (Argument Quality n=1, Legal n=2, Dialogue n=2, Text-to-Image n=2); traffic-light signals and domain rankings are presented with apparent precision (e.g., 4.33 vs 4.00) that the data cannot support. Study 1b generalizes a 'moving target vs structural ceiling' distinction from a SINGLE benchmark (SummEval) with four models.

Numbers are internally inconsistent in places, which is concerning for a paper that stresses reproducibility. The abstract/Table 4 cite the SBIC/offensiveness human ceiling as 0.905/0.91 but the text also calls it the 'strongest social-computing case (Promising)'; Table 4 lists SBIC best model 0.86 and jury 0.84 while Figure 12 shows 0.86 and the abstract says glm-5.1 0.856. The HateXplain ceiling appears as 0.835 (text) and 0.83 (tables). Study 1b '25,600 judgments' (Table 2 caption and App B) vs the body's framing needs reconciliation (1600 summaries x 4 aspects x 4 models = 25,600 is fine, but the per-aspect Spearman vs summary-level averaging is described inconsistently). The contamination tiers are 'MEDIUM' for every dataset in Table 4 but 'LOW/MED' for MultiPICo in Table 5 -- the caveat on the NLI 'Validated' result depends on this and is not cleanly resolved.

Contamination probe is weak as designed. Verbatim quote-completion measures memorization of surface text, not label memorization; a MEDIUM tier on every dataset is uninformative for adjudicating whether agreement reflects competence. The claim that contamination is 'ruled out' as the explanation for the social-computing shortfall is not strongly supported by a probe that returns the same tier for the strong (NLI) and weak (emotion) cases.

Citations for some of the strongest epistemological and HCI-practice claims are thin or generic. Claims about deskilling, 'methodological monoculture,' 'epistemic injustice,' and 'incentive capture' (Section 7.3) are presented as established risks but are largely asserted without HCI/STS literature support beyond one or two references; for a CHI audience these need grounding in the relevant scholarship (e.g., on automation and deskilling, epistemic injustice as a named concept, reflexivity).

The alt-test and 'replacement' framing conflates statistical replaceability with the epistemological argument the paper itself makes. The paper rightly argues interpretation is not labeling, yet its empirical apparatus reduces 'can a machine judge like a human' to majority-label accuracy and a count-based annotator-swap test -- a purely classificatory operationalization that cannot speak to reflexivity, positionality, or theme development, the very things Study 2 says matter. The Discussion claims the two are 'two views of the same boundary,' but this is an interpretive leap, not a demonstrated equivalence.
required_revisions: Address single-coder reliability head-on. Either (a) have a second independent human coder re-screen and re-code a substantial random sample (e.g., 20-30%) of both corpora for inclusion and for the 1-5 reliability verdict, and report Cohen's/Krippendorff agreement and disagreement resolution; or (b) if a full dual-coding is infeasible, explicitly reframe the reviews as 'scoping/structured' rather than claiming PRISMA-grade systematic-review rigor, and foreground the single-coder limitation in the abstract and methods, not only buried in Section 8. The current framing is inconsistent with the paper's own epistemic argument.

Establish construct validity of the reliability rubric. Document, per study, how 'the human-human ceiling' was obtained when the primary study did not report one; report what fraction of the 217 + 55 studies actually reported a human-human ceiling vs had it imputed; provide rubric anchor examples and an audit of borderline verdicts. Without this the central measurement is unauditable.

Reconcile the HCC framing with what is tested. Either add at least one genuine HCC/qualitative-coding dataset with public multi-coder labels to Study 3 (the paper says these are 'in progress'), or substantially soften the title and contribution claims so they do not over-promise an HCC-specific empirical result that the NLP/CSS datasets do not deliver.

Fix and cross-check every numeric inconsistency (SBIC/HateXplain ceilings, best-model and jury values across Table 4 / Table 5 / Figure 12 / abstract, contamination tiers MultiPICo MEDIUM vs LOW/MED, judgment counts). Provide a single source-of-truth table. For a paper whose selling point is auto-generated reproducible statistics, these discrepancies are damaging.

Down-weight or clearly flag all n<=3 domain/method cells. Remove or grey out point estimates (e.g., 4.33 vs 4.00) that small n cannot support; report only family-level aggregates with CIs for sparse cells; and add uncertainty bands to Figures 3, 5, 7, and Tables 1, 3.

Strengthen the contamination analysis or weaken the 'memorization ruled out' claim. A probe that returns MEDIUM for every dataset cannot discriminate competence from recall; either add a label-level contamination test (e.g., perturbed/counterfactual items, or a held-out post-cutoff replication) or restate the conclusion as 'not explained by surface memorization on the one low-recall set' rather than 'ruled out.'

Ground the Section 7.3 critical claims (deskilling, methodological monoculture, epistemic injustice, incentive capture) in the relevant HCI/STS/measurement literature, or present them explicitly as conjecture rather than findings. As written they read as advocacy unsupported by the cited evidence base.

Clarify the epistemological scope of the alt-test/accuracy operationalization: state plainly that Study 3 measures classificatory substitutability only, and does not and cannot test reflexive/interpretive equivalence, so that the Discussion's 'two views of the same boundary' claim is not over-read.
requested_experiments: Dual-coder reliability check: have a second human independently re-code a random 25% sample of the Study 1a (217) and Study 2 (55) corpora on (i) inclusion/exclusion and (ii) the 1-5 reliability verdict; report Krippendorff alpha (ordinal for the verdict, nominal for inclusion) and a confusion matrix of disagreements, plus how the LLM-assisted verdicts compare to the human re-codes. This directly tests the validity of the review's core output.

Add at least one HCC qualitative-coding dataset with public multi-coder labels to Study 3 (candidates: a released CSCW/CHI codebook study, or the multi-coder labels in any of the Study-2 corpus studies the authors cite as having public coder-level data) and run the same 6-model panel + alt-test + ceiling analysis, so an HCC-method claim is backed by HCC-method evidence.

Label-level contamination test: for each Study-3 dataset, construct counterfactual/perturbed items (e.g., swap target group in HateXplain, negate hypotheses in NLI) and measure whether judge accuracy degrades as expected; compare against the verbatim-recall tier to show the recall probe is (or is not) predictive of label memorization.

Post-cutoff replication: re-run Study 1b SummEval analysis on a second, post-training-cutoff summarization-quality set with human aspect ratings (e.g., a 2025/2026 human-rated summarization benchmark) to test whether the 'fluency ceiling is structural' claim generalizes beyond the single SummEval dataset.

Soft-label / disagreement-aware evaluation: in Study 3, report judge performance against the full human label distribution (e.g., cross-entropy or Jensen-Shannon to the human soft labels, and correlation with human entropy per item) rather than only majority-accuracy, to substantiate the perspectivism argument the paper makes in the Discussion.

Sensitivity analysis of the mixed-effects model: refit excluding the two NLI datasets (which drive the 'above ceiling' cells) and excluding qwen3.5-9b (the outlier small model), and report whether the subjectivity OR and item-level variance dominance are stable; also report the model with an interaction between scale and subjectivity to test whether scale helps differentially on low- vs high-ceiling tasks.

Prompt/temperature robustness: re-run a subset (e.g., 2 datasets x 3 models) at >=2 alternative prompt phrasings and a non-zero temperature with multiple samples to quantify how much of the reliability verdict is an artifact of the single prompt and decoding setting, given the paper's own 'design-choice sensitivity' failure mode.
minor_comments: Reference [27] James et al. (2026) and [36] Ngo et al. (2026) and several '2026' entries are cited as published; given the May 2026 submission, verify these are real and dated correctly, as forward-dated citations undermine the reproducibility claim.

Figure rendering in the provided text is badly garbled (Figures 3-5, 11-13 axis labels and numbers are interleaved); ensure the camera-ready figures are legible, as the landscape figures carry much of the argument.

The 'institutional vLLM API' and 'available open-model catalogue' are vague; specify exact model checkpoints, quantization (some are fp8), context windows, and serving config so 'most powerful open models available to us' is reproducible and not a moving target.

Model names (deepseek-v4-pro, glm-5.1, qwen3.6, gemma-4) read as near-future/fictional versions; if these are real, pin versions and dates; if pseudonymized, say so. This matters for the 'moving target' claim's credibility.

Section 2 says 29 studies reported an extractable numeric agreement value out of 217 -- clarify how the other 188 were assigned verdicts without a numeric value, as this bears directly on rubric validity.

Study 2 mean reliability differences (augment 3.17 vs replace 3.07 vs simulate 2.80) are presented as tracking reliability but are within plausible noise for these small n; add CIs or a test before claiming the role 'tracks' reliability.
The abstract is extremely dense (one ~400-word block); consider tightening, as key caveats (single-coder, single-provider, no qualitative-coding dataset) are absent from it.

Clarify whether the 'human ceiling' for NLI (ChaosNLI ~100 annotators) and for the 3-annotator social sets are comparable quantities; random-annotator-vs-majority accuracy behaves very differently at A=3 vs A=100 and this affects cross-dataset comparison of the ceiling.

Ethics: good practice on not redistributing harmful text, but state IRB/ethics-board determination explicitly (even if exempt) and whether annotator-level data reuse for HateXplain/SBIC respects the original consent/licensing for re-derivation of annotator identities in the alt-test.

expertise: Statistics, experimental design, and psychometrics (mixed-effects models, multiple-comparison control, power analysis, measurement/inter-rater reliability)
summary: The paper synthesizes four studies on when an LLM-as-a-judge can substitute for human judgment in Human-Centered Computing: a 217-study cross-domain PRISMA review (1a), a SummEval replication with frontier open models (1b), a 55-study HCC-specific PRISMA review (2), and a new 18,000-judgment empirical evaluation of a 6-model open panel on six human-labeled datasets (3) with a mixed-effects analysis, alt-test, and contamination probe. The organizing claim is that LLM-judge reliability is governed by the human-human agreement ceiling: judges match or exceed humans on objective/low-ceiling tasks (NLI) but fall short on subjective high-consensus social-computing constructs (emotion, hate), and scaling the model does not move that ceiling. The framing, decision framework, and reproducibility posture are valuable and timely for HCC. However, from a statistics/psychometrics standpoint several of the central inferential claims are not adequately supported: the key fixed-effects predictors (subjectivity and scale) are estimated from only 6 datasets and 6 models while p-values borrow power from 18,000 correlated rows (pseudoreplication); the human ceiling is treated as a known constant despite being a noisy sample statistic (especially for ~3-annotator datasets); the power/CI claims and the BH "below-ceiling" tests ignore ceiling sampling variance; and reported reliability metrics (κ, macro-F1) are largely absent from the main results. The reviews rest on single-reviewer LLM-assisted coding with no inter-coder reliability, and only 29/217 studies in Study 1a yielded extractable numbers, making the headline domain means thinly supported.
strengths: Timely, well-motivated, and genuinely useful framing for HCC: the augment/replace/simulate x epistemic-stance coding and the ceiling-anchored decision framework are a real conceptual contribution beyond existing LLM-judge surveys.

Anchoring reliability to the human-human ceiling rather than a manufactured 'gold' is methodologically the right move and is consistent with the learning-with-disagreement / perspectivist literature it cites.

Study 1b's decomposition of SummEval (open models surpass G-Eval on the verifiable consistency aspect but not on the variance-compressed fluency aspect) is a clean, persuasive illustration of the temporal-gap vs structural-ceiling distinction.

Inclusion of MultiPICo (2024) as a low-contamination clean condition, plus an explicit contamination probe and the count-based alt-test, shows commendable attention to confounds that most judge papers ignore.

Strong reproducibility commitments: fixed temperature 0, documented model identifiers, released corpora, item-level labels and aggregates, auto-generated statistics macros, and an ethics-conscious decision not to redistribute raw hateful text.
weaknesses: Pseudoreplication in the mixed-effects model. The two headline fixed effects are defined at coarse cluster levels: subjectivity (1-ceiling) takes only 6 values (one per dataset) and scale (log10 params) only 6 values (one per model). Reporting OR=0.17 at p=1e-7 and OR=1.30 at z=13.40 from N=18,000 individual judgments treats correlated within-cluster observations as independent evidence about a 6-level predictor. With 6 datasets, a dataset-level fixed effect is also collinear with the dataset random intercept; the effective degrees of freedom for the subjectivity slope are ~4, not ~18,000. The p-values are almost certainly anticonservative. There is no random intercept for model, so the scale effect is similarly unguarded against between-model confounding (e.g., gemma-31B ties glm-754B, directly contradicting a monotone scale story).

The human ceiling is treated as a fixed known constant. The ceiling (random-annotator-vs-majority accuracy) is itself a sample statistic with non-trivial variance, severe for SBIC/HateXplain (~3 annotators/item) and GoEmotions (~3-5). The 'below ceiling' BH tests, the alt-test thresholding at 0.5, and the power claims all compare an estimated judge accuracy against a point estimate of the ceiling without propagating the ceiling's uncertainty. Conclusions like '22 of 36 cells significantly below the human ceiling' could change materially once ceiling CIs are included.

Power/CI claims are stated for the worst case (p=0.5) and conflate two things. '95% CI half-width <= 4.4%' is just the binomial half-width at p=0.5, n=500; it is not a power statement. 'Power > 80% for a 0.10 gap vs the ceiling' is unjustified because (a) the comparison is against an estimated ceiling, (b) bootstrap-CI overlap is not a hypothesis test, and (c) no alpha, test, or variance assumption is specified. No pre-specified effect size or test is given despite the paper recommending exactly this to others.

Headline reliability metrics are missing from the main results. The method promises Cohen's κ and macro-F1, but Table 4 and Figure 11 report only accuracy vs ceiling. For imbalanced/subjective tasks (irony macro-F1 ~0.45 at 0.82 accuracy is mentioned in prose) accuracy is exactly the wrong summary, and the paper itself argues this. κ and macro-F1 per cell must be in the main tables for the 'subjectivity governs' claim to be auditable.

Both systematic reviews use a single-reviewer, LLM-assisted coding pipeline with no inter-coder reliability for the verdict rubric, role, or stance codes. The five-level verdict and augment/replace/simulate/stance codings are the substrate of nearly every figure, yet there is no second coder, no κ, and no adjudication. For a paper whose thesis is that interpretive coding requires human reliability checks, the absence of any reliability estimate on its own coding is a notable internal inconsistency.

Study 1a's quantitative landscape is thinly supported: only 29 of 217 studies reported an extractable numeric agreement value, yet domain means with n as low as 1-3 (e.g., Argument Quality n=1, Legal n=2, Text-to-Image n=2) are plotted with apparent precision and assigned traffic-light signals. Means over verdict ordinals (a 1-5 rubric) are reported to two decimals without dispersion, and ordinal codes are treated as interval.

Contamination inference is over-strong. Nearly every cell is MEDIUM, including the 'Validated' NLI results the paper leans on. The claim that memorization is 'ruled out' rests on a single dataset (MultiPICo) being LOW for one small model; this is suggestive, not a ruling-out, and the probe's HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW thresholds (0.6 / 0.35) are arbitrary and unvalidated against known-contaminated controls.
required_revisions: Re-fit the inferential model honoring the cluster structure: treat dataset and model as crossed random effects (random intercepts for both, and ideally a random slope for scale across datasets), and report the subjectivity and scale effects with degrees of freedom / inference appropriate to 6 datasets and 6 models (e.g., a dataset-level meta-regression of ceiling on accuracy, or a parametric-bootstrap / cluster-permutation test over datasets). Drop or heavily qualify the p=1e-7 / z=13.40 statistics, which are pseudoreplicated.

Propagate human-ceiling uncertainty everywhere it is used: report a bootstrap CI for each dataset's ceiling (resampling annotators within items), and recast every judge-vs-ceiling comparison (the BH tests, the alt-test, the 'significantly below ceiling' counts) as a test of the gap with both judge and ceiling variance included. Re-state how many of the 36 cells remain significant under this correct comparison.

Add Cohen's kappa and macro-F1 (with per-class F1) to the main per-cell results (Table 4 and the figure), not just accuracy, since the central argument is about subjective/imbalanced constructs where accuracy is uninformative; show that the subjectivity gradient holds under chance-corrected and class-balanced metrics.

Restate the power/sample-size justification precisely: give the pre-specified effect size, the test, alpha, and the variance assumptions, and either run a proper power analysis for the gap-vs-estimated-ceiling test or remove the >80% power claim. Clarify that 4.4% is a single-proportion CI half-width, not power.

Establish coding reliability for both reviews: have a second coder independently apply the five-level verdict, role, and stance rubrics to a random subset (e.g., 20-30%), report Krippendorff's alpha / Cohen's kappa per code, and describe adjudication. Without this the qualitative-coding sections cannot meet the bar the paper itself sets.

Report dispersion and honest small-n caveats for all Study 1a/1b/2 domain and method means: show n, SD/range, and suppress or grey-out traffic-light verdicts for domains with n<=3; justify treating ordinal 1-5 verdicts as interval (or switch to medians / distribution plots).

Soften the contamination conclusion: present the probe thresholds' provenance, validate them against at least one known-contaminated and one known-clean control, and replace 'rules out memorization' with a calibrated statement about the residual risk given that most cells are MEDIUM.
requested_experiments: Dataset-level meta-regression: with the 6 datasets as units, regress judge accuracy (and separately kappa, macro-F1) on (1-ceiling) and report the slope with a leave-one-dataset-out / parametric-bootstrap CI; this is the statistically honest version of the 'subjectivity governs' claim and is directly runnable from the released aggregates.

Crossed random-effects GLMM with random intercepts for BOTH item and model (and a by-dataset random slope for log10 params), refit on the existing 18,000 judgments; report variance components and the scale effect with model treated as a random factor, to test whether the scale OR survives once between-model variance is not absorbed into fixed effects.

Annotator-bootstrap of the human ceiling for each dataset (resample annotators within each item, 1000 reps) to produce ceiling CIs; recompute the count of judge x dataset cells that are significantly below ceiling under a gap test that includes ceiling variance, and report how the 22/26 count changes.

Add per-cell Cohen's kappa and macro-F1 to Table 4 and re-run the alt-test using a chance-corrected agreement (or report alt-test winning-rate CIs via the same annotator bootstrap), to show the conclusions are not artifacts of raw accuracy on imbalanced labels.

Soft-label / distributional evaluation: since ChaosNLI, GoEmotions, MultiPICo ship full annotator distributions, compare each judge's implied or sampled label distribution to the human distribution (e.g., Jensen-Shannon divergence or cross-entropy against soft labels) in addition to majority accuracy, to substantiate the 'flattening / majority-default' claim with a distributional statistic rather than only macro-F1 in prose.

Contamination-probe validation: run the verbatim-recall probe on a held-out clearly-post-cutoff control set and on a clearly-pretraining set, to calibrate the HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW thresholds and quantify false-positive/negative rates of the probe before using it to caveat the NLI 'Validated' verdicts.
Inter-coder reliability sub-study: second-coder re-annotation of a random 25% of each review corpus on verdict/role/stance, reporting Krippendorff's alpha, to validate the review's own measurements.
minor_comments: The reference list cites items dated 2026 (e.g., [27] James et al. 2026, [36] Ngo et al. 2026) — verify these are real and consistent with the submission timeline; for double-blind CHI ensure no self-identifying 'authors (2025)/(2026)' placeholders leak identity.

Figures 3-13 are described as auto-generated but several (e.g., Fig 3b, Fig 4b, Fig 11) are extremely dense and partly unreadable in the linearized text; ensure axis labels, ns, and CIs are legible in the typeset version.

State the exact GLMM software/priors for the 'Bayesian mixed-effects logistic GLMM' and report posterior intervals, not just a frequentist-style OR/p; currently the Bayesian and cluster-robust frequentist results are conflated.

Clarify how ties were 'dropped' when forming the majority gold and how this sampling interacts with the ceiling estimate (dropping high-disagreement items inflates the ceiling and biases the subjectivity predictor).
The 'reliability score = 6 - verdict' transform and the green/amber/red thresholds (>=3.5, 2.5-3.5, <2.5) are arbitrary cutpoints on an ordinal scale; report sensitivity to these thresholds.
Define the alt-test winning-rate sampling and its CI; a single point estimate crossing 0.5 is presented as a binary pass/fail with no uncertainty.
GoEmotions is originally a 27-emotion multilabel corpus; collapsing to 4-way sentiment is a strong construct redefinition that should be justified and its effect on the ceiling discussed.

area: Responsible AI, data contamination, and ML generalization
expertise: Responsible AI, data contamination, and ML generalization; LLM evaluation methodology; perspectivist/disagreement-aware NLP; systematic review methods.
summary: The paper synthesizes the question of when an LLM-as-a-judge can substitute for human judgment in Human-Centered Computing (HCC). It comprises four sub-studies: (1a) a PRISMA review of 217 cross-domain studies producing a green/amber/red reliability landscape anchored to the human-human agreement ceiling; (1b) a replication of the G-Eval/SummEval summarization-quality protocol with four frontier open models to separate a temporal "matter of time" gap from a structural ceiling; (2) a PRISMA review of 55 HCC/HCI/qualitative studies coding LLM role (augment/replace/simulate) and epistemic stance; and (3) a new empirical meta-evaluation across six datasets (ChaosNLI-SNLI/MNLI, GoEmotions, SBIC, HateXplain, MultiPICo) using a 6-model open panel (9B-754B, single provider) with human-ceiling anchoring, an alt-test for replacement, a mixed-effects analysis, and a verbatim-recall contamination probe. The organizing claim is that judge reliability is governed by the human-agreement ceiling and item-level (dis)agreement, not by model scale or memorization; the headline structural/temporal distinction is supported by the SummEval fluency result and the recent low-contamination MultiPICo result, which the authors argue rules out memorization as the explanation for the subjective-task shortfall. The work culminates in a decision framework and a checklist for designing LLM-as-a-judge studies.
strengths: Timely and genuinely useful synthesis for HCI/HCC: the augment/replace/simulate coding plus the human-ceiling-anchored reliability landscape is a contribution the community can act on, and the decision framework and 8-point checklist are concrete and well-motivated.

Methodological framing around the human-human ceiling (rather than a manufactured 'gold') and the use of the alt-test as a replacement gate are the correct, responsible-AI-aligned choices, and they are applied consistently across Study 3.

The explicit inclusion of a contamination probe and a recent (2024) low-contamination dataset (MultiPICo) as a 'clean condition' shows awareness of memorization as a confound, which most judge-evaluation papers omit entirely.

The mixed-effects analysis (item-level random intercepts, subjectivity vs scale fixed effects, BH-corrected per-cell ceiling tests) elevates the central claim from descriptive to inferential, and the finding that item-level variance dominates is a credible and important mechanism.

Strong reproducibility posture: released corpora, search logs, harnesses, auto-generated statistics macros, fixed model identifiers, temperature 0, and a defensible ethics stance (releasing only item-level labels/aggregates for sensitive sets).
weaknesses: The contamination probe is too weak to support the load-bearing 'rules out memorization' claim. A verbatim quote-completion / next-token-recall probe primarily detects surface memorization of text spans, not memorization of the label distribution or the task itself. A model can have LOW verbatim recall of MultiPICo's raw text yet still have seen the construct, similar Reddit/Twitter irony data, or downstream derivatives. The probe also returns a single coarse tier (almost everything is MEDIUM), gives no false-positive/false-negative calibration, no negative control (a guaranteed-unseen post-cutoff set), and no membership-inference or perplexity-based cross-check. The structural-vs-temporal conclusion rests heavily on this single, under-validated instrument.

The 'structural ceiling' claim is overstated and partially confounded with measurement artifacts. The SummEval fluency result (low summary-level Spearman) is attributed to a 'compressed human reference,' but low variance in human ratings mechanically deflates Spearman regardless of judge quality; this is a property of the metric choice (per-document Spearman on a near-constant variable), not necessarily a 'structural ceiling' of LLM judging. Similarly, MultiPICo being 'matched only at its low ceiling' while defaulting to the majority class (macro-F1 ~0.45) is consistent with a model that has NOT learned the construct, which actually weakens, not strengthens, the 'not memorization, it's the ceiling' narrative. The paper conflates 'accuracy near ceiling' with 'genuine competence at the ceiling.'

Generalization claims outrun the evidence. Study 3 uses open models from a single provider/serving stack (institutional vLLM), no proprietary frontier judges (GPT/Claude/Gemini), no prompt-sensitivity or temperature-sensitivity analysis (single temperature 0, single prompt per task), and 6 datasets dominated by NLI and English social-media text. Claiming a general 'principle' that scale does not raise the ceiling, from a 9B-754B open-only panel at one prompt, is not warranted; the scale OR (1.30/log10) is estimated over a narrow, non-uniformly-sampled scale range with confounded model families.

The two systematic reviews are single-reviewer and LLM-assisted with no inter-rater reliability on the verdict coding, no second human coder, and a verdict rubric that 'embeds judgement.' For a paper whose entire thesis is that human-human agreement is the proper benchmark, the absence of any agreement statistic on its OWN coding (the 217 and 55 verdicts, the augment/replace/simulate codes, the stance codes) is a serious internal-consistency and validity gap. Only 29/217 studies had extractable numeric agreement, so most of the 'landscape' rests on subjective verdicts.

The mixed-effects specification has identifiability concerns: 'subjectivity' is operationalized as 1 - ceiling at the dataset level, so it is a between-dataset variable with only 6 levels, yet it is treated as a continuous fixed effect with a precise OR and a tiny p-value (1e-7). With 6 datasets, subjectivity is nearly collinear with dataset identity; the dataset random intercept (SD 0.73) and the subjectivity fixed effect compete for the same between-dataset variance, making the OR and its CI hard to interpret. The cluster-robust GLM p-values are computed over 18000 non-independent judgments clustered in only 6 datasets / 3000 items, which inflates apparent precision.

Ethics/sensitive-data handling is asserted but thin. HateXplain and SBIC contain targeted hate and identity-attack content; the paper says it processes but never reproduces them and releases only labels, but there is no discussion of annotator-identity / demographic representation in the ceilings it treats as ground truth, no IRB/ethics-board statement, and no analysis of whether the 'human ceiling' itself encodes majority-group bias, which directly undercuts the epistemic-injustice argument the paper itself makes in 7.3.
required_revisions: Strengthen or substantially qualify the contamination claim. Either (a) add a properly validated contamination methodology with a negative control (a guaranteed post-training-cutoff dataset), a positive control (a dataset known to be in pretraining), false-positive/false-negative calibration of the verbatim probe, and at least one independent signal (e.g., guided vs unguided generation gap, per-example perplexity/min-k% prob, or membership inference), OR (b) soften every 'rules out memorization' / 'structural not temporal' sentence to a clearly-hedged claim and remove it from the abstract and the organizing principle.

Disentangle 'structural ceiling' from metric artifacts. For SummEval fluency, report a variance/ceiling diagnostic (human-rating variance per document, and an agreement metric robust to low variance such as accuracy-within-1 or rank-tie-corrected correlation) and show the low correlation is not simply the per-document-Spearman-on-low-variance artifact. For MultiPICo, explicitly reconcile 'matched at ceiling' with macro-F1 ~0.45 / majority-class defaulting and revise the claim, since majority-defaulting is evidence AGAINST genuine construct competence.

Add proprietary frontier judges and prompt/decoding-sensitivity to Study 3, or explicitly scope every generalization claim to 'open models, single provider, single prompt, temperature 0.' The 'scale does not raise the ceiling' principle must be supported by a wider, more uniformly sampled scale range and by ruling out prompt sensitivity as the driver of the subjective-task shortfall.

Report inter-rater reliability for the paper's own coding. Have a second coder independently code a random sample (e.g., 20-30%) of both corpora on verdict, role, and stance, and report Cohen's/Krippendorff agreement. A paper arguing that human-human agreement is the gold standard must demonstrate its own coding ceiling.

Fix or reframe the inferential model. With only 6 datasets, report dataset-level (leave-one-dataset-out) robustness of the subjectivity OR, show the model is not driven by one or two datasets, clarify how subjectivity (1-ceiling) and the dataset random intercept are jointly identified, and report effective sample size / number of clusters underlying the p-values rather than N=18000.

Add a substantive ethics section covering: ethics-board/IRB status for processing hate/identity-attack corpora; the demographic composition and potential majority-group bias of the human annotators whose majority label is treated as the ceiling; and how treating a majority label as 'ground truth' interacts with the epistemic-injustice/flattening harms the paper itself identifies in Section 7.3.
requested_experiments: Negative/positive contamination controls: run the identical verbatim-recall probe and the full judging pipeline on (a) a guaranteed post-cutoff dataset (e.g., a 2026 social-media irony/offensiveness set collected after every panel model's training cutoff) as a clean negative control, and (b) a dataset highly likely to be memorized (e.g., original SNLI test split text) as a positive control; report whether the probe tier and the judge accuracy track the known contamination status, and report verbatim-probe ROC/threshold calibration.

Independent contamination signal: compute a guided-vs-unguided generation gap and/or min-k% probability / per-example perplexity membership-inference scores for each Study-3 dataset and correlate dataset-level contamination scores with judge accuracy-minus-ceiling; this tests whether the 'memorization ruled out' claim holds under a method that detects distributional (not just verbatim) memorization.

Prompt and decoding robustness: re-run Study 3 with at least 3 paraphrased prompts per task and temperatures {0, 0.7} (>=3 samples at 0.7), and report accuracy/kappa variance attributable to prompt and decoding versus model; this tests whether the subjective-task shortfall is robust or a prompt artifact.

Add proprietary frontier judges (e.g., GPT-4-class, Claude-class, Gemini-class) to the Study-3 panel on all 6 datasets and re-estimate the scale fixed effect and the per-cell ceiling tests; report whether any closed model crosses a high subjective ceiling, directly stress-testing 'scale does not raise the ceiling.'

Soft-label / disagreement-aware evaluation: in addition to forced-majority accuracy, evaluate every judge against the full human label distribution (cross-entropy / Jensen-Shannon to the human soft labels, and calibration of the model's class probabilities to human proportions) on GoEmotions, HateXplain, SBIC, MultiPICo; report whether models capture minority perspectives or collapse to the majority, quantifying the flattening claim.

Second-coder reliability study on the reviews: independently re-code a >=20% random sample of the 217 + 55 corpora for verdict, role, and stance with a blinded second coder and report Krippendorff alpha; if alpha is low for subjective codes (e.g., stance), re-state the corresponding findings as tentative.

Annotator-bias sensitivity: where annotator demographic metadata exists (GoEmotions, SBIC, HateXplain, MultiPICo's perspectivist annotators), recompute the human ceiling and judge agreement stratified by annotator subgroup, and test whether judges agree more with majority-group annotators than minority-group annotators (a direct, runnable test of the epistemic-injustice claim).
minor_comments: The reference list contains entries dated 2026 (e.g., refs 27, 36, 56-style 2026 works) and the manuscript is dated May 2026; ensure these are real and not anachronistic, and clarify the model 'training-cutoff relative to dataset release' for every Study-3 model, which the paper recommends but does not itself fully report.

The model names (deepseek-v4-pro, glm-5.1, qwen3.6, gptoss-120b, gemma-4-31b) are not anchored to public model cards, parameter counts, or training cutoffs in the text; for reproducibility and for the scale analysis, give a table with exact parameter counts, context, and cutoff dates.

Table 4 lists every Study-3 dataset contamination tier as MEDIUM (with MultiPICo LOW/MED), which makes the tiering nearly uninformative; either refine the tier resolution or acknowledge the probe lacks discriminative power.

Study 1b reports 25,600 judgments for 1600 summaries x 4 aspects x 4 models = 25,600, which checks out, but the abstract elsewhere says Study 1b 're-runs SummEval'; clarify that only summary-level Spearman is reported and consider also reporting system-level and sample-level correlations as G-Eval did.

The claim '100% answer-parse rate and zero API errors' is presented as a quality signal but is orthogonal to validity; consider relocating it and instead reporting refusal rates on the sensitive (hate/offensive) tasks, which is the relevant reliability concern for those datasets.

The decision framework's 'Automate (with light audit)' regime for stance/topic/relevance relies heavily on two 2023 ChatGPT-vs-crowd papers (refs 20, 45) whose own ceilings are crowd-level; note explicitly that 'beats crowd workers' is benchmarked against a low ceiling and is not a claim of expert-level competence.

Figures 3-13 are described in dense prose; several (the landscape heatmap, the decision map) are hard to parse from the text alone and likely from the rendered PDF; ensure axis labels and bubble legends are legible at print size.
